argue.definition: To disagree with someone, often with strong feelings.
argue.example: It is normal for families to argue sometimes.

resolved.definition: Dealt with and finished; found a solution.
resolved.example: The argument was resolved after they talked calmly.

successful.definition: Achieving what you wanted; working well.

acknowledged.definition: Recognized and accepted as true or real.
acknowledged.example: Her feelings were acknowledged by her partner.

context.definition: The situation or background around something.
context.example: What you are arguing about is the context.

process.definition: The steps you take to do something.
process.example: The process of solving a problem matters as much as the answer.

tackle.definition: To deal with or try to solve a problem.

heated.example: The discussion became heated when nobody listened.

lobbing.definition: Throwing something back and forth casually.
lobbing.example: They kept lobbing reasons at each other.

workable.example: They found a workable solution to their problem.

compromise.definition: An agreement where each side gives up something.
compromise.example: A good compromise makes both people feel heard.

typical.example: This is a typical family argument.

server.definition: In tennis, the person who hits the ball first; here, the person who starts the argument.
server.example: The server made the first point in the argument.

responder.definition: The person who answers or replies.
responder.example: The responder had a quick answer for every reason.

hardens.definition: Becomes hard and difficult to remove.
hardens.example: Food hardens on the dishes if you leave them.

unhygienic.example: Leaving dirty dishes out is unhygienic.

accumulated.definition: Collected or built up over time.
accumulated.example: Bacteria accumulated on the dirty plates.

bacteria.definition: Very tiny living things that can sometimes cause sickness.
bacteria.example: Bacteria grow on food that is left out.

deemed.example: The person who ran out of reasons was deemed the loser.

opponent.definition: The person you are fighting or competing against.
opponent.example: Your partner should not feel like an opponent.

expense.definition: The cost or harm caused to someone.
expense.example: Winning an argument at the expense of your partner is not worth it.

distraction.definition: Something that takes your attention away from what matters.
distraction.example: Changing the subject is a form of distraction.

appropriate.definition: Right or suitable for the situation.
appropriate.example: It may be appropriate to delay the conversation.

delay.definition: To wait before doing something; to put off.
delay.example: Sometimes it helps to delay a difficult conversation.

altogether.example: Avoiding conflict altogether is not healthy.

intimacy.definition: A close, warm connection between people.
intimacy.example: Avoiding conflict can also mean avoiding intimacy.

taboo.definition: A subject that people do not talk about because it feels forbidden.
taboo.example: Too many subjects became taboo in their family.

skirting.example: Politely skirting around problems makes things lonely.

martyr.definition: A person who suffers or does everything themselves to make others feel guilty.
martyr.example: Being a martyr does not help your relationships.

guilty.definition: Feeling bad because you did something wrong or think you did.
guilty.example: The martyr wants to make everyone feel guilty.

resentful.definition: Feeling angry because something seems unfair.

persecutor.definition: A person who attacks or treats others badly.

insults.definition: Rude or hurtful words said to someone.
insults.example: Flinging insults during an argument hurts everyone.

flings.example: She flings insults when she is angry.

atmosphere.definition: The feeling or mood in a place.
atmosphere.example: Arguments create a bad atmosphere at home.

threatens.example: Arguing threatens a child's sense of safety.

emergency.definition: A sudden, serious situation that needs quick action.
emergency.example: Their focus switches into emergency mode.

ideal.example: What is the ideal way to argue?

grouches.definition: Complaints or things that bother you.
grouches.example: Don't save up your grouches and pour them out at once.

blaming.definition: Saying someone else is the cause of a problem.
blaming.example: Start with how you feel, not with blaming.

thumb.definition: In 'rule of thumb', a simple, practical guide.
thumb.example: A good rule of thumb is to use I-statements.

pigeon-holed.definition: Put into a narrow category that does not fit well.
pigeon-holed.example: Nobody likes to be pigeon-holed by someone else.

guaranteed.example: No way of arguing is guaranteed to work perfectly.

manipulation.definition: Controlling someone in a clever or unfair way.
manipulation.example: Good relationships are not about manipulation.

manipulating.definition: Trying to control someone by tricking or pressuring them.
manipulating.example: Manipulating someone does not build a good connection.

owning.definition: Taking responsibility for your own feelings or actions.
owning.example: Owning your feelings helps your relationships.

promotes.definition: Helps something grow or become more common.

adopt.definition: To start using or doing something.
adopt.example: Children will adopt the arguing style they see at home.

emotionally.definition: In a way that relates to feelings.
emotionally.example: Emotionally intelligent people understand others' feelings.

intelligent.definition: Able to understand and learn things well.
intelligent.example: An emotionally intelligent person handles conflict well.

inspecting.example: He was inspecting his dad's old leather jacket.

leather.definition: A strong material made from animal skin, used for clothes and bags.
leather.example: His dad's old leather jacket was still in good shape.

nerve.definition: A sensitive point; something that upsets you easily.
nerve.example: The comment hit a nerve and made him angry.

possessions.example: He did not like his son eyeing up his possessions.

dispute.example: This isn't a serious dispute, just a small argument.

truce.example: They laughed and reached a truce.

liable.example: If they don't understand, something similar is liable to happen again.

mediator.definition: A person who helps two sides find an agreement.
mediator.example: The mediator helped them understand each other's feelings.

retaliation.definition: Doing something bad to someone because they did something bad to you.
retaliation.example: He saw his father's response as retaliation.

counter-attacked.example: Feeling attacked, he counter-attacked with an insult.

defensively.definition: In a way that shows you feel you need to protect yourself.
defensively.example: He defensively replied that his son called him old.

cling.definition: To hold on tightly to something because you do not want to let go.
cling.example: He wanted to cling to symbols of his youth.

symbols.definition: Things that stand for or represent something else.
symbols.example: The jacket was a symbol of his younger years.

youthful.example: Clinging to youthful clothes was a comfort to him.

comfort.definition: A feeling of being relaxed and free from worry.
comfort.example: The old jacket gave him comfort.

unpack.definition: To examine something carefully and explain it piece by piece.
unpack.example: Let's unpack what really happened in that argument.

meta.definition: Looking at something from a higher, bigger-picture view.
meta.example: Take a meta perspective to see the full situation.

perspective.definition: A way of looking at or thinking about something.
perspective.example: Try to see the situation from the other person's perspective.

protagonist.definition: The main person in a story or situation.
protagonist.example: Work out the feelings of each protagonist.

dialogue.definition: A conversation between two or more people.
dialogue.example: Think about how to change the dialogue.

recap.definition: A short summary of what was said before.
recap.example: Here is a quick recap of the main points.

imminent.example: She could tell an argument was imminent.

advocating.example: I am not advocating that you lose all spontaneity.

vulnerability.definition: Being open about your weaknesses or fears.
vulnerability.example: Embrace your vulnerability instead of hiding it.

mask.definition: To hide or cover up something.
mask.example: He tried to mask his fear with anger.

projecting.definition: Putting your own feelings onto someone else.
projecting.example: Try not to project your feelings onto the other person.

intent.definition: The purpose or plan behind what someone does.
intent.example: Don't assume the intent of the other person.

cornerstone.definition: The most important part of something.
cornerstone.example: Understanding feelings is the cornerstone of good relationships.

foundation.definition: The base that everything else is built on.
foundation.example: Good communication is the foundation of a healthy family.

empathic.definition: Able to understand and share other people's feelings.
empathic.example: Empathic parenting helps children feel safe.

negotiations.definition: Discussions where people try to agree on something.
negotiations.example: Understanding feelings is important in negotiations.

swiftly.example: Improvements in communication came pretty swiftly.

patterns.definition: Ways of doing things that happen again and again.
patterns.example: Good patterns of relating can improve quickly.
